coredata.dc:title: DFT study on the effect of functional groups of carbonaceous surface on ammonium adsorption from water
coredata.dc:creator: Yin, Qianqian
Si, Linhui
Wang, Ruikun
Zhao, Zhenghui
Li, Heping
Wen, Zhengcheng
coredata.dc:description: Density functional theory (DFT) was used to study the adsorption of ammonium ion on carbon materials. The effects of single and multiple adjacent functional groups of carbon structures on ammonium ion adsorption were emphasized. The electrostatic potential, adsorption energy, charge transfer, molecular orbital, and dipole moment of different configurations were analyzed. Results showed that the carbonyl group was more likely to adsorb ammonium ion than lactone, carboxyl, and hydroxyl. When the carbon material contained multiple adjacent functional groups at the same time, the adsorption of ammonium ion can be promoted or inhibited due to the interaction among functional groups. The effect of functional groups on the adsorption of π bond in carbon materials was related to the electronegativity of functional groups, i.e., greater electronegativity led to smaller adsorption energy of π bond. Carbon material itself is nonpolar and hydrophobic, so adding oxygen-containing functional groups can increase the dipole moment of carbon material molecules, thereby enhancing its polarity and adsorption capacity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ammonium ion
DFT
Carbonaceous surface
Adsorption
Oxygen functional groups

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Frontier molecular orbitals and dipole moment
Quantum theory of atoms in molecules

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Molecular electrostatic potential maps of different carbon structures.
Results of adsorption of ammonium ion by carbon structures containing a single functional group.
Results of adsorption of ammonium ion by carbon structures containing multiple functional groups.
Adsorption result of π bond on carbon structure for ammonium ion.
Adsorption of ammonium ion on carbon structures containing a single functional group.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: (*) represents the adsorption site, and the bond distance is the distance between nitrogen atom and the adsorption site.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Adsorption properties of ammonium ion on carbon structures containing multiple functional groups.
Adsorption characteristics of π bond on carbon structure for ammonium ion.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The bond distance is the distance between the center of the carbon ring and the nitrogen atom.

head.ce:title: DFT study on the effect of functional groups of carbonaceous surface on ammonium adsorption from water

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Power Engineering, North China Electric Power University, 619 Yonghua North Street, Baoding, 071003. China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Density functional theory (DFT) was used to study the adsorption of ammonium ion on carbon materials. The effects of single and multiple adjacent functional groups of carbon structures on ammonium ion adsorption were emphasized. The electrostatic potential, adsorption energy, charge transfer, molecular orbital, and dipole moment of different configurations were analyzed. Results showed that the carbonyl group was more likely to adsorb ammonium ion than lactone, carboxyl, and hydroxyl. When the carbon material contained multiple adjacent functional groups at the same time, the adsorption of ammonium ion can be promoted or inhibited due to the interaction among functional groups. The effect of functional groups on the adsorption of π bond in carbon materials was related to the electronegativity of functional groups, i.e., greater electronegativity led to smaller adsorption energy of π bond. Carbon material itself is nonpolar and hydrophobic, so adding oxygen-containing functional groups can increase the dipole moment of carbon material molecules, thereby enhancing its polarity and adsorption capacity.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon materials are extensively used as adsorbent in pollution remediation because of their rich pore structure, large specific surface area, good adsorption performance, and stable chemical properties. Various substances such as heavy metals, volatile organic compounds (VOCs), and phenols, can be effectively adsorbed by carbon materials (Liu et al., 2020; Abdelsalam et al., 2019). The surface chemical property of carbon material is an important factor affecting its adsorption performance, which is largely determined by the category and number of surface functional groups. Lactone, hydroxyl, carboxyl, and carbonyl are the most common oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of carbon material (Jia et al., 2002). Most of these oxygen-containing functional groups are negatively charged, thus, they easily adsorb positively charged ammonium ion. Yin et al. (2018) proved that the property of functional groups on the surface of carbon materials is an important factor affecting the adsorption of ammonium ion on carbon materials. Zeng et al. (2013) found that CO and –COO functional groups could react with ammonium ion. These studies concluded that the existence of functional groups can promote the adsorption of ammonium ion on carbon materials. However, obtaining the specific adsorption form and adsorption site of ammonium ion on carbon materials with functional groups based on experimental study is difficult. The microscopic mechanism of adsorption has not been fully studied at the molecular level.

In recent years, quantum chemical calculation has become an important tool to study the structure and properties of molecules. For most small and medium molecule systems, detailed information on molecular energy, charge, dipole moment, chemical bond, and other characteristics can be obtained through quantum chemical calculation. The calculation results have important guiding significance for the study of adsorption mechanism and the development of experimental research. Accordingly, increased attention has been paid to the interaction between adsorbents and adsorbates at the atomic level through quantum chemical calculation. The adsorption capability of hydrated Cd and Pb on graphene quantum dots has been investigated by Abdelsalam et al. (2018a) through DFT calculations. Reisi Vanani et al. (Reisi Vanani and Alihoseini, 2014) substituted two and four nitrogen atoms into corannulene and investigated their structures, analyzing the effect of nitrogen substitution in carbon nanostructures on molecular hydrogen adsorption at the theoretical level of MP2/6-31G (d). Siddiqui et al. (2016) evaluated the potential of scrap rubber tire as a cheap adsorbent to remove nickel ion from aqueous solution through experiments and DFT calculation and subsequently analyzed the influence of a single functional group on nickel ion adsorption. Researchers have analyzed the effects of functional groups on the adsorbability of carbon materials through quantum chemical calculation. However, focus has been given mostly on the effects of a single functional group on the adsorbability of carbon materials through quantum chemical calculation. In fact, the surface of carbon materials is rich in oxygen-containing functional groups, commonly including carboxyl, hydroxyl, carbonyl, and lactone, and multiple functional groups often coexist (Salame and Bandosz, 2001; Bandosz et al., 1996). However, there is little research on the quantum chemical calculation of ammonium ion adsorption on carbon material. Particularly, the effect of functional groups on carbon material on the adsorption of ammonium ion, which is essentially important, has rarely been considered. Therefore, to be more consistent with the actual surface properties of carbon materials, a carbon material model with multiple functional groups is necessary to establish in the study of ammonium adsorption on carbon materials.

In the current work, the adsorption of ammonium ion on a model of carbon materials containing functional groups from water was analyzed based on the DFT. The effects of the number and type of functional groups in the carbon structure on the adsorption of ammonium ion were determined from the aspect of molecular electrostatic potential (MEP), adsorption energy, charge transfer, frontier molecular orbital, dipole moment, density-of-states (DOS) and quantum theory of atoms in molecules (QTAIM). The adsorption mechanism was investigated at the microlevel.

DFT is a favorable theory for calculating structural properties due to its good balance between computational efficiency and accuracy (He et al., 2015). In this study, the density functional method B3LYP, which is widely adopted in quantum chemical calculation, is used to optimize the structure of the calculation model, It can well represent the electronic structure of C-based nanosystems and can describe the relationship between the molecular bonds (Lee et al., 1988). The water solution model was used in the reaction conditions to calculate the solvation effect. The 6-31G (d, p) base group could meet the requirement of precision of results for a calculation model containing only C, H, O, and N atoms. Calculation is performed using Gaussian09 software package (Frisch et al., 2016). Convergence is based on software defaults. The B3LYP method is not enough to reasonably describe the weak interaction between molecules. DFT-D3 is used to correct the weak interaction during adsorption (Yan et al., 2020; Gao et al., 2020). Adsorption energy is an important index to evaluate the adsorption process. The calculation equation of system energy is as follows (Chi et al., 2006; Padak and Wilcox, 2009):
E ads
= E
( AB )
-
( E
( A )
+ E
( B )
)

E
ads is the adsorption energy, E(AB) is the total energy after adsorption, and E(A) and E(B) are the optimized total energy of adsorbent and adsorbate, respectively. Adsorption is of two types: if the value of E
ads is lower than −50 kJ/mol, it is physical adsorption; otherwise, it is chemical adsorption (Yan et al., 2020). Basically, larger adsorption energy means easier adsorption.

In the present study, carbon rings from a single layer graphite structure were used to simulate carbonaceous surface models. This model has been successfully applied to many studies with favorable results (Gao and Yang, 2016; Gao et al., 2018). The carbon material structure reportedly comprises randomly connected graphite clusters containing 12–25 carbon atoms (3–7 carbon rings) (Chen and Yang, 1998). In the current work, a cluster structure of seven carbon ring is constructed as a carbon material model. Geometric optimization is performed first. The average C–C and C–H bond lengths are 1.424 and 1.087 Å, respectively. The comparison of the optimization results of the carbon structure with the experimental values is listed in Table S1. The calculated results well agree with the experimental values under the theoretical level of B3LYP/6-31G (d, p), indicating that the selection of this model is scientific and reasonable.

Many types of functional groups exist on the surface of carbon materials. Oxygen-containing functional groups primarily include carboxyl, hydroxyl, carbonyl, and lactone (Jia et al., 2002; Salame and Bandosz, 2001; Bandosz et al., 1996). In this study, carbon structures containing a single functional group and several kinds of functional groups were constructed. The adsorption energy, bond distance, charge of ammonium ion (Q), total dipole moment (TDM) of carbon structures, density-of-states (DOS) and quantum theory of atoms in molecules (QTAIM) are calculated. The effects of the existence of a single functional group and the coexistence of multiple functional groups on the properties of carbon structure and the adsorption properties of ammonium from water are discussed.

Relative electron density is an important aspect to discuss in adsorption. MEP can be used to analyze the relative electron density in molecules, thereby providing partial reactivity information of molecules in the study (Raghi et al., 2018). By analyzing the MEP diagram, the possible positions and directions of the given reactants or formed complex can be predicted (Hakiri et al., 2018). Fig. 1 shows the MEP diagrams of carbon structures containing different functional groups and the color related to charge density. Table S2 shows the MEP values of different functional groups of carbon structures. From the results of MEP diagrams and MEP values, the strongest adsorption sites of ammonium ion on carbon structures can be observed. On the MEP diagram, the blue area is the positively charged region and the red or orange area is negatively charged. Ammonium ion has a positive charge in the aqueous solution, so the electrostatic potential diagram of the ammonium ion is all colored blue in Fig. 1(j). The hydrogen atom in the carboxyl group and that in the hydroxyl group are also shown in blue in Fig. 1(c) and (d), respectively, indicating their positively charged nature. An effective attraction of the negatively charged area in carbon structure easily occurs to the positively charged ammonium ion. In other words, the red area in the carbon structure has a tendency for absorbing ammonium ion.

For the carbon structure with a single functional group, the red orange region near the carbonyl oxygen is the darkest, followed by the lactone group, and that for the hydroxyl group is the lightest. On the carboxyl group, the color near the carbonyl oxygen is also darker than that near the hydroxyl oxygen. It can be seen from Table S2 that the MEP value of carbonyl group is the largest (−1.98 eV) among the carbon structures containing single functional group, followed by lactone group −1.97 eV, and that of carboxyl group and hydroxyl group are −1.55 eV and −0.89 eV, respectively. Therefore, the electronegativity of the functional group may decrease in the order of carbonyl > lactone > carboxyl > hydroxyl. The charge property of the carbon structures with multiple functional groups significantly changes from that with a single functional group. We speculate that mutual inhibition may exist between different groups. For example, the color near the carboxyl group in Fig. 1(h) is much lighter than that of a single carboxyl functional group in Fig. 1(c). Charge intensification can also be found between different functional groups. For example, the red region in Fig. 1(g) is intensified compared with that in Fig. 1(a) and (c) because of the coexistence of carboxyl and carbonyl functional groups. In addition to the functional group area, the rest of the carbon ring area should be noted. The hydrogen atoms around the carbon ring are shown in light green, indicating that they are weakly positive. They may still interact with ammonium ion through the electron cloud generated by the sp2 hybridization orbital (Ribeiro et al., 2019). In Fig. 1(i), the color of the two carbon rings far from the functional groups appears white, indicating that the electron cloud generated by the sp2 hybridization orbital is relatively weak. This finding may be due to the interaction between different functional groups. The analysis results of electrostatic potential diagram provide reference for the selection of initial adsorption sites. From the above analysis, the calculation results can be obtained quickly and accurately when the adsorption site of the ammonium ion adsorption calculation is selected near the functional groups with dark red and orange. Another kind of adsorption sites of interest is near the carbon ring with an electron cloud produced by the sp2 hybrid orbital, i.e., the π bond adsorption site.

The strength of adsorption capacity is related to the type of adsorption bond. Adsorption energy can be used to measure the adsorption capacity of adsorbent to adsorbates. Generally, the adsorption bond is stronger and the energy is larger in chemical adsorption than in physical adsorption (Zhou et al., 2019). The effect of functional groups in the carbon structure on ammonium ion adsorption can be determined by analyzing the adsorption energy. The adsorption characteristics for ammonium ion adsorption onto carbon structures with single and multiple functional groups are listed in Tables 1 and 2
, respectively. The π bond adsorption results are listed in Table 3 . The corresponding visualized adsorption results can be found in Figs. 2–4
.

According to the calculated results, the adsorption energy between ammonium ion and functional group modified carbon materials ranges within −20 kJ/mol to −70 kJ/mol. The relatively low adsorption energy indicates that physical adsorption and weak chemical adsorption may exist at the same time. Physical adsorption generally refers to the mutual attraction between adsorbents and adsorbates through intermolecular forces (van der Waals force and hydrogen bonding) (Rajter and French, 2010). László, K. (László, 2005) found that the adsorption of phenol on oxidized carbon materials was primarily attributed to the hydrogen bond between oxygen-containing functional groups and phenol. Chemical adsorption is usually accompanied by electron transfer, atomic rearrangement or destruction and formation of chemical bonds between adsorbents and adsorbates. Generally speaking, physical adsorption and chemical adsorption are intertwined (Liu et al., 2018). Abdelsalam et al. (2019) studied the adsorption of hydrated heavy metals such as lead and chromium on graphene, and found that physical adsorption and chemical adsorption existed simultaneously. It is found in Tables 1–3 that each adsorption situation is accompanied by electron transfer. A high charge transfer amount corresponds to a high adsorption energy. There is no atomic rearrangement or destruction from the corresponding visualization results. But for several models, such as 7C–CO and 7C–COCOOH, the adsorption energy values are greater than −50 kJ/mol, which still belong to chemical adsorption.

The data in Table 1 show that the adsorption energy values of carbon structures with different functional groups to ammonium ion vary. Among them, the adsorption of ammonium ion to CO bond (such as carbonyl, lactone, and carbonyl on carboxyl) has higher adsorption energy values, whereas that to the C–O bond (such as hydroxyl and the hydroxyl on carboxyl group) has lower adsorption energy values. Thus, the adsorption energy of ammonium ion with carbon structures is sensitive to the properties of functional groups.

The adsorption of ammonium ion onto carbonyl groups shows higher adsorption energy and shorter bond distance than that to other functional groups, indicating that carbonyl oxygen is more likely to absorb ammonium ion. According to the adsorption energy data in Table 1, the influence of different functional groups on the adsorption of ammonium ion onto carbon materials decreases as follows: carbonyl > lactone > carboxyl (carbonyl oxygen) > hydroxyl > carboxyl (hydroxyl oxygen). This finding is in accordance with the depth of the red orange color areas of the carbon structure containing different functional groups in Fig. 1. The adsorption energy of ammonium ion to the dark color areas is greater than that to the light color one.

Table 2 shows that when two functional groups are adjacent and exist at the same time, the adsorption energy is generally greater than that of a single functional group. The adsorption energy of carbon structure with adjacent carbonyl, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups is the highest, reaching −67.31 kJ/mol. The carbon structure with adjacent carbonyl, carboxyl, hydroxyl, and lactone groups has the minimum adsorption energy of −43.51 kJ/mol for ammonium ion, as shown in Table 2(g). The adsorption energy of structure in Table 2(c) is significantly higher than that of the structure in Table 1(c). When the carbon structure contains adjacent carbonyl, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups at the same time (Table 2(d)), adsorption energy increases by about 10 kJ/mol than that containing only carbonyl (Table 1(a)). However, the adsorption energy in Table 2(e) is smaller than that in Table 1(a), although they have almost the same adsorption distance. These results indicate that the interaction among different functional groups may influence one another and then inhibit or promote the adsorption of ammonium. This result is consistent with the findings from the MEP diagram. As shown in Fig. 3(d), when carbonyl, carboxyl and hydroxyl groups coexist and adjacent to each other, adsorption is optimized with the initial adsorption site near the carbonyl oxygen or hydroxyl oxygen of carboxyl, respectively. In these two cases, ammonium ion finally adsorbs near the carbonyl group. Thus, ammonium ion crosses the hydroxyl group and prefers to adsorb onto the carbonyl group, also indirectly proving that the hydroxyl group has little influence on the adsorption of ammonium ion. For the carbon structure with adjacent carbonyl, hydroxyl, carboxyl, and lactone simultaneously, three adsorption sites of the carbonyl oxygen in carbonyl, lactone, and carboxyl are selected, and the calculation results are shown in Fig. 3(e)–(g), respectively. Although three adsorption sites show the same adsorption distance of 2.70 Å, they have different adsorption energies.

The adsorption sites of ammonium ion on the surface and on the edge of the carbon ring without functional groups are studied, and results as shown in Fig. 4(a). The calculation and visualization data reveal that the ammonium ion eventually adsorbs onto the surface of the carbon structure in both cases, and adsorption distance and adsorption energy are the same. Thus, ammonium ion is more likely to adsorb onto the surface of the carbon structure when the carbon structure does not contain a functional group.

Carbon materials are usually rich π electrons, so the interaction between adsorbate and π electron is very important (Siddiqui et al., 2016). In this study, the effects of different functional groups on the adsorption of ammonium ion by π bond are discussed. The same carbon ring is selected in the carbon structure containing different functional groups when calculating the π bond adsorption. As listed in Table 3, the adsorption energy of the π bond of the carbon structure containing hydroxyl group and ammonium ion is higher than that of the carbon structure without a functional group by 0.64 kJ/mol. This finding indicates that the presence of hydroxyl group promotes the π bond adsorption of carbon materials for ammonium ion. When carbonyl, carboxyl, or lactone groups exist solely on the carbon structure, the π bond adsorption energy is lower than that of the carbon structure without a functional group. The existence of these functional groups on the carbon structure inhibits the adsorption of ammonium ion by the π bond on the carbon structure. The π bond adsorption of ammonium ion on the carbon structure containing only lactone shows the smallest adsorption energy value, which is 3 kJ/mol less than that on the carbon structure without a functional group. The order of influence on the adsorption energy of the π bond is lactone > carbonyl > carboxyl > hydroxyl. The presence of functional groups on the carbon structure affects π bond adsorption because it reduces or increases the electron-cloud density generated by the carbon ring through the sp2 hybridized orbital (Li et al., 2015). A higher electron-cloud density corresponds to greater electrostatic attraction to the positively charged ammonium ion, and vice versa. Carbonyl, carboxyl, and lactone groups are electron absorbing groups. Their presence reduces the density of the electron-cloud on the carbon ring, resulting in the π bond adsorption energy being about 1–3 kJ/mol smaller than that of the carbon structure without functional groups. Hydroxyl group is a weakly electron donating group and increases the density of the electron-cloud on the carbon structure, making the π bond adsorption energy slightly larger than that of the carbon structure without functional groups. A comparison of Table 3 with Tables 1 and 2 reveals that a larger adsorption energy value of the carbon structure containing a single functional group leads to a smaller corresponding π bond adsorption energy value.

Charge distribution was analyzed by the Mulliken layout. The amount of charge transfer is calculated by Q = Qtotal - Qin (Qtotal is the total charge of adsorbent after adsorption, and Qin is the total charge of adsorbent before adsorption) (Abdelsalam et al., 2019). The calculation results are shown in Tables 1–3. A negative value of Q means that the electron charge is transferred from the carbon material to the ammonium ion, i.e., the ammonium ion is the electron acceptor, and the carbon material is the electron donor. Oxygen-containing groups on the surface of the carbon material interact with ammonium ion to give and accept electrons, which is a very important way in adsorption (Yang et al., 2018).

A high charge transfer amount corresponds to a high adsorption energy and a small adsorption distance. A large adsorption distance generally represents physical adsorption, whereas a small one represents chemical adsorption (Gao et al., 2018). The adsorption of ammonium ion onto the hydroxyl oxygen in carboxyl in Table 1 shows a small amount of charge transfer of 0.072e, which corresponds to a large adsorption distance of 2.85 Å and a small adsorption energy of −34.66 kJ/mol. The carbon structure containing solely carbonyl transfers a large amount of electrons of 0.121e because a chemical bond forms between ammonium ion and carbonyl group, so the adsorption structure is strong. Thus, the corresponding adsorption distance is small and adsorption energy is high, with values of 2.67 Å and −57.32 kJ/mol, respectively. As shown in Table 2(a)–(c), when two functional groups coexist on the carbon structure, the amount of charge transfer and adsorption energy significantly increase. This finding is consistent with the results of MEP diagram analysis. In other words, the adsorption capacity of the dark red region is greater than that of the light color one. As shown in Table 3, all π bond adsorptions show small amounts of charge transfer with an adsorption distance of about 3.1 Å. The adsorption energy is smaller than −50 kJ/mol, which means the physical adsorption occurs between π electron and ammonium ion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Frontier molecular orbitals and dipole moment
ce:section.ce:para: The highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO)–lowest occupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy gap is a characteristic observation of molecules. A higher HOMO energy level means a lower ionization energy, indicating that the cluster molecules are more likely to lose electrons. LUMO is the lowest vacant orbital, and a lower energy level means greater ease for cluster molecules to capture electrons. The HOMO energy (EHOMO) and LUMO energy (ELUMO) are calculated, and the HOMO-LUMO gap (Egap) value is determined by Egap = ELUMO-EHOMO. Egap reflects the ability of electrons to transition from an occupied orbital to a vacant one. A higher Egap means a lower likelihood of electron transition and generally more stable cluster molecules (Abdelsalam et al., 2018a).

The dipole moment is the value obtained by multiplying the distance between the centers of the positive and negative charges (d) by the charge (q) in the center. It is often used to evaluate the spatial configuration and the polarity of molecules. Greater dipole moment means greater polarity of the molecular cluster (Reisi Vanani and Alihoseini, 2014). Carbon materials are nonpolar adsorbents that are not easily soluble in water. They only show adsorption capacity for various nonpolar organic substances in aqueous solution. The polarity of carbon materials is increased by introducing surface functional groups, enabling it to have enhanced adsorption properties (Zhou et al., 2020).

According to the data in Table S2, when no functional group is added, the centers of the positive and negative charges coincide and the dipole moment is zero due to the symmetry of the carbon structure. The charge in the carbon structure is transferred after the addition of functional groups. The centers of the positive and negative charges do not coincide, and the dipole moment increases. Oxygen atom differs from carbon atom in electronegativity. Therefore, the addition of oxygen-containing groups to the carbon structure results electron transfer and increased TDM. The change in dipole moment is related to the nature of the functional groups. Dipole moment increases by 1.24 (D) after adding hydroxyl to carbon structure, and the value is 5.65 (D) for lactone. The influence of the functional groups on the dipole moment of carbon structure decreases in the order of lactone > carbonyl > carboxyl > hydroxyl. This finding is consistent with the result of the influence of functional groups on the π bond adsorption energy. The hydroxyl group has the least effect on the dipole moment of the carbon structure because the hydroxyl group has the least charge transfer in the carbon structure. Similarly, the lactone group has the greatest charge transfer in the carbon structure. For carbon structures containing multiple functional groups, the dipole moment values also increase compared with the corresponding carbon structures containing a single functional group. As shown in Fig. 3(g), the dipole moment increases from 5.79 (D) to 39.75 (D) after adsorption.

Table S2 shows that the carbon structure without functional group has a high Egap of 4.04 eV. After adding a functional group, Egap of the carbon structure decreases, indicating that the presence of functional group increases chemical activity. This finding is consistent with the result of adsorption energy from Tables 1 and i.e., the presence of functional groups increases the adsorption activity of ammonium ion in the carbon structure. The addition of functional groups makes the electron cloud on HOMO/LUMO orbit transfer. As shown in Fig. S1, the carbon structure without functional groups is compared with that having functional groups, the electron-cloud density on the HOMO/LUMO orbit has changed. On the LUMO orbital, the electron-cloud density shifts to the carbonyl, carboxyl, and lactone, respectively, and the electron-cloud density does not change significantly for the hydroxyl group. The density of electron cloud formed on HOMO/LUMO orbit is consistent with the molecular electrostatic potential. The influence of functional group on the energy gap decreases in the order of carbonyl > lactone > carboxyl > hydroxyl. Egap also changes before and after adsorption. For example, Egap decreases from 3.30 eV to 2.55 eV after adsorption of ammonium ion by carbon structure containing carbonyl oxygen. The Egap decrease is due to the inclusion of new energy states to the energy spectrum caused by the interaction between carbonyl oxygen and ammonium ion (Perveen et al., 2020). Egap decreases by 0.75 eV after ammonium ion adsorption on the carbon structure containing only carbonyl. This finding may be related to the chemical adsorption between carbonyl and ammonium ion. The weak adsorption between hydroxyl and ammonium ion results in a slight decrease in Egap by 0.03 eV. For carbon structures with multiple functional groups, Egap generally drops by about 0.5 eV after adsorption.

The DOS analysis was conducted on the adsorption of
NH 4 +
on four single functional group models and their corresponding π bond adsorption, and the results are shown in Fig. S2. Each vertical line represents a molecular orbital, and red and blue lines represent the contribution of two fragments of
NH 4 +
and carbon structure to the total density-of-states (TDOS), respectively. The green lines represent the overlap population density-of-states (OPDOS), which is useful to examine the interactions between fragments (Gao et al., 2020; Hussain et al., 2019; Abdelsalam et al., 2018b).

As shown in Fig. S2, when
NH 4 +
is adsorbed on the carbonaceous surface, carbon structure mainly contributes to the TDOS from −0.8 a.u. to 0.2 a.u..
NH 4 +
also makes contribution to TDOS between - 0.7 a.u. and - 0.5 a.u., 0 a.u. and 0.2 a.u., indicating that there are few electrons occupied by carbon atoms in the molecular orbital. For all adsorption models, the OPDOS is close to zero, which indicates that there is barely orbital hybridization between the two molecules. For all adsorption models, the OPDOS is negative from 0.1 a.u. to 0.2 a.u.. This result indicates a negative effect on the bonding between
NH 4 +
and carbon structure in the high energy region of the molecular orbital. However, the OPDOS is positive near −0.6 a.u. for all functional group adsorption models, which indicates a positive effect on the bonding between
NH 4 +
and carbon structure in the low energy region of the molecular orbital.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum theory of atoms in molecules
ce:section.ce:para: QTAIM is a useful tool for visualizing chemical interactions and their nature, including non-covalent interactions. In a QTAIM analysis, the nature of the interaction could be described through some parameters, such as Laplacian of electronic density (∇2ρ(r)) and the total electronic energy (H(r)) of the bond critical point. Negative values of ∇2ρ(r) indicate covalent bonds, while positive values indicate non-covalent bonds (Keith et al., 1996). When both the values of ∇2ρ(r) and H(r) are positive, the nature of the interaction is electrostatic. When the value of ∇2ρ(r) is positive and that of H(r) is negative, the interaction is partially covalent (T Reis et al., 2020). The values of the topological parameters for the interactions are shown in Table S3. All the values of ∇2ρ(r) are positive, which indicate non-covalent bonds for all complexes. The values of H(r) are negative for the configurations with adsorption energy greater than −50 kJ/mol, which indicates that the interactions are partially covalent. Thus, this result is consistent with that of the adsorption energy.

According to the above results, the carbon structure containing hydroxyl group has the weakest ability to adsorb ammonium ion, whereas that containing carbonyl group has the strongest ability to adsorb ammonium ion. Generally, multiple functional groups on the carbon structure are conducive to the adsorption of ammonium ion. The π bond adsorption is weakened when functional groups exist on the carbon structure. Saleh et al. (2013) studied the adsorption of lead ion by pyrene molecules, which are functionalized by carbonyl, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups. Calculation results show that lead ion is more likely to be adsorbed in the presence of solely carbonyl group than in any other situation. The adsorption of lead ion onto CO is more stable than that on –OH. Siddiqui et al. (2016) used DFT to study the adsorption of the heavy metal nickel ion by using functionalized pyrene molecules. They found that nickel ion is more likely to adsorb onto carbonyl oxygen atoms compared with hydroxyl oxygen, and that the π bond adsorption is weak. Therefore, carbon materials containing carbonyl groups have a strong adsorption capacity for metal ion, whereas those containing hydroxyl oxygen have relatively weak adsorption ability. Ammonium ion has metal properties, so these conclusions can be satisfied when the carbon structure containing functional groups adsorbs ammonium ion.

DFT is used to study the adsorption performance of ammonium ion on carbon materials, focusing on the influence of functional groups and the π electron of carbon material on ammonium ion adsorption. The electronegativity of the functional group is determined by MEP analysis to decrease in the order of carbonyl > lactone > carboxyl > hydroxyl. The adsorption site of ammonium ion is near the oxygen atom of the functional group. The adsorption ability of the carbon structure for ammonium ion is enhanced after adding functional groups to the carbon structure. The adsorption ability of the carbon structure containing a single functional group decreases in the order of carbonyl > lactone > carboxyl > hydroxyl. In general, the carbon structure containing multiple functional groups promotes adsorption because of the interaction among different functional groups. The π bond adsorption of ammonium ion weakens when different functional groups exist on the carbon structure. Adding functional groups to the carbon structure can change its polarity and cause it to have high adsorption ability for ammonium ion. Therefore, the results of this study have important guiding significance for the surface modification of carbon materials and applications in ammonium ion adsorption from water.

Qianqian Yin：Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Writing - Review & Editing. Linhui Si: Formal analysis, Data Curation, Writing-Original Draft. Ruikun Wang: Methodology, Conceptualization, Writing - Review & Editing. Zhenghui Zhao: Writing - Review & Editing. Heping Li: Data Curation, Writing - Review & Editing, Resources. Zhengcheng Wen: Software, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant No.51506054), the Hebei Provincial Natural Science Foundation (grant No. E2016502089), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (grant No. 2021MS074).
